rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe distress but afebrile and haemodynamically explained by pain, with a reassuring lower-tract screen (no flank pain, still voiding, negative nitrites/leukocytes). Telehealth cannot examine the abdomen, test urine or scan the bladder, and the patient is an overdose risk having maxed OTC analgesia — so prompt in-person urinalysis and review (urgent, not emergency) is proportionate. T2 is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 for fever/flank pain, anuria >6h, or clots.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever with shivering, or pain moving to the mid-back/flank
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests pyelonephritis/obstructed infected system — risk of urosepsis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever and shivering, or the pain moves to your mid-back, go to the Emergency Department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Completely stopping passing urine for more than 6 hours, or passing blood clots
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to ED / call for urgent care
escalation_edges.rationale: Acute or clot retention needs urgent catheterisation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you completely stop passing urine for more than 6 hours or start passing blood clots, go to the Emergency Department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling unable to keep safe or tempted to take extra medication
escalation_edges.rationale: Overdose risk in a catastrophising patient requires timely support and safe medication management.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel you might take extra medication or can't cope, tell your spouse and call for help now — a crisis line or your after-hours service.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Involve the spouse in monitoring, safe medication storage, and arranging same-day assessment.
Manage as a flare with multimodal non-opioid measures while excluding infection/obstruction.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is most likely a flare of your bladder condition, not an infection, but because we can't test your urine over a chat you need a urine test and in-person review soon. Use heat, avoid bladder irritants, don't take any more of your usual painkillers today, and have your spouse stay with you.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Arrange a prompt in-person GP/after-hours review with a urine test; use ED for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If the emergency features appear after hours, use ED.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the 12-hour flare, afebrile, no flank pain, still voiding, and that urinalysis/MCS is needed.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Self-care/reassurance without any infection/obstruction screen or urine test, missing pyelonephritis or an obstructed infected system.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An occult upper-tract infection progresses to urosepsis after phone-only reassurance.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Immediate ambulance for an afebrile, stable IC/BPS flare with a reassuring screen, over-medicalising a non-emergency.
